content:
Feature-engineering **encoding workflows** matured as tabular ML moved from hand-built **dummy variables** in spreadsheets to **sklearn Pipelines** and **ColumnTransformers** that bundle heterogeneous preprocessing with estimators.

Competition-era **Kaggle** pipelines popularized per-column encoding menus—one-hot for low-cardinality nominals, **mean encoding** for cities and ids—with post-mortems showing that **leakage-safe fitting** mattered as much as encoder choice. That lesson split **definitions** (now centralized under data preprocessing) from **engineering discipline** (this topic).

**FeatureHasher** and **DictVectorizer** extended hashing strategies from text to high-cardinality categoricals. Boosting libraries (**XGBoost**, **LightGBM**, **CatBoost**) added **native categorical** support, changing when teams skip one-hot entirely.

Modern MLOps practice treats the **serialized Pipeline**—encoders plus model—as the deployable unit, with encoding decisions documented alongside schema contracts for incoming categorical fields.

timeline.label: Spreadsheet dummy variables
timeline.description: Analysts manually create indicator columns per category before regression—precursor to automated one-hot encoders.

timeline.label: sklearn ColumnTransformer
timeline.description: Unified API for applying different encoders per column inside composable pipelines.

timeline.label: Competition encoding menus
timeline.description: High-cardinality target and frequency encodings drive workflow-focused best practices and leakage awareness.

timeline.label: Native categorical boosters
timeline.description: Tree libraries accept categorical dtypes directly, adding model-aware branches to encoding workflows.
